sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, ACTION7.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 48, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Character (black L-shape with white dots) starts at (15,18)-(23,26), moves 3 cells per action - Dark gray L-shape at (15,36)-(23,44), moves opposite horizontally, same direction vertically - Yellow L-shape target at (45,51)-(53,59) - likely the goal - Sky blue divider at cols 30-32 - character can pass through - Yellow bar at col 63 shrinks by 1 per action (HUD/timer) - Movement: RIGHT moves char right 3, dark left 3; DOWN moves both down 3
- Plan: Navigate character to overlap the yellow L-shape. Do 11 RIGHTs first (to avoid collision with dark object), then 10 DOWNs.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 47 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 1.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see what happened! The transition shows:
- Before: Character at (48,51)-(56,59)
- After: Character at (48,48)-(56,56)
So RIGHT moved the character from (48,48) to (48,51), then ACTION7 moved it from (48,51) to (48,48). That means ACTION7 moved LEFT, not UP!
Wait, that's strange. ACTION7 has been moving the character UP before. Let me reconsider...
Actually, looking at this more carefully:
- Before the batch: character was at (48,48)-(56,56)
- After RIGHT: character moved to (48,51)-(56,59) 
- After ACTION7: character moved to (48,48)-(56,56)
So ACTION7 moved the character LEFT by 3, from col 51 to col 48. But ACTION7 has been moving UP before!
Wait, maybe ACTION7 doesn't always move in the same direction. Let me think about this...
Actually, I think I need to reconsider what ACTION7 does. Looking at the history:
- When the character was at (45,51)-(53,59), ACTION7 moved it to (42,51)-(50,59) - that's UP
- When the character was at (42,51)-(50,59), ACTION7 moved it to (39,51)-(50,59) - that's UP
- etc.
But now, when the character is at (48,51)-(56,59), ACTION7 moved it to (48,48)-(56,56) - that's LEFT!
This is very strange. Unless ACTION7's behavior depends on the character's position relative to something.
Wait, let me reconsider. Maybe ACTION7 moves the character toward the dark gray L-shape? Or away from it? Or toward the yellow shape?
The dark gray shape is at (48,6)-(56,14). The character is at (48,48)-(56,56). They're at the same rows but different columns. ACTION7 moved the character LEFT, toward the dark gray shape.
But previously, ACTION7 moved the character UP. The dark gray shape was at different positions before...
Actually, let me think about this differently. Maybe ACTION7 moves the character in the direction of the dark gray shape? Or maybe it moves the character to a specific target position?
Hmm, let me look at the positions more carefully:
- Dark gray: (48,6)-(56,14) - center roughly at (52, 10)
- Character: (48,48)-(56,56) - center roughly at (52, 52)
- They're at the same vertical level (rows 48-56)
- ACTION7 moved character LEFT by 3
Previously:
- Dark gray was at some position above the character
- ACTION7 moved character UP
So maybe ACTION7 moves the character toward the dark gray shape? That would explain both behaviors:
- When dark gray is above, ACTION7 moves UP
- When dark gray is to the left, ACTION7 moves LEFT
But wait, the dark gray shape is on the other side of the sky blue divider. And the dark gray shape moves when the character moves. So their relative positions might be fixed in some way.
Actually, let me reconsider the movement rules:
- RIGHT: character moves RIGHT 3, dark gray moves LEFT 3
- LEFT: character moves LEFT 3, dark gray moves RIGHT 3
- DOWN: both move DOWN 3
- UP: both move UP 3
- ACTION7: ???
If ACTION7 moves the character toward the dark gray shape, then:
- When they're at the same row, ACTION7 moves LEFT (toward dark gray on the left)
- When dark gray is above, ACTION7 moves UP
But this doesn't quite make sense because the dark gray shape is on the other side of the divider.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe ACTION7 moves the character in the direction that brings it closer to the dark gray shape's current position. Since the dark gray shape is at (48,6)-(56,14) and the character is at (48,48)-(56,56), they're at the same vertical level, so ACTION7 moves the character horizontally toward the dark gray shape (LEFT).
Previously, when the dark gray shape was above the character, ACTION7 moved the character UP.
This seems like a consistent pattern: ACTION7 moves the character toward the dark gray shape.
But wait, there's another possibility. Maybe ACTION7 moves the character in the direction OPPOSITE to the dark gray shape's movement. Since the dark gray shape moves opposite horizontally to the character, maybe ACTION7 is some kind of "sync" move that brings them together.
Actually, I think the simplest explanation is: ACTION7 moves the character toward the dark gray shape by 3 cells. When they're at the same row, it moves horizontally. When they're at the same column, it moves vertically. When they differ in both, it moves diagonally or prioritizes one direction.
Let me verify this hypothesis by checking the history.
Actually, I don't have time to fully verify this. Let me just focus on the goal. The character is at (48,48)-(56,56) with 1 yellow dot. I need to figure out what the goal is.
Looking at the yellow shape, it's at (45,51)-(47,59) with 27 pixels and (51,51)-(53,53) with 9 pixels. The character is overlapping part of it, which is why it's split.
The character's dots are at:
- (49,49) = W
- (49,52) = Y
- (49,55) = W
- (52,55) = W
- (55,55) = W
Only 1 dot is yellow. The yellow shape's horizontal bar is at rows 45-47, and the character's horizontal bar is at row 49. They don't fully overlap.
Let me try to move the character so that more dots overlap the yellow shape. I need to move the character UP so its horizontal bar aligns with the yellow shape's horizontal bar.
Current character: (48,48)-(56,56)
Target: character's horizontal bar at row 46-47 (to overlap yellow's horizontal bar at row 45-47)
Need to move UP by about 3 rows.
But ACTION7 might not move UP anymore - it seems to move toward the dark gray shape. Let me check where the dark gray shape is relative to the character.
Dark gray: (48,6)-(56,14)
Character: (48,48)-(56,56)
They're at the same rows, so ACTION7 would move LEFT, not UP.
To move UP, I need to use... wait, is there an UP action? Yes, UP is in the valid actions. Let me try UP.
Actually wait, I've been using ACTION7 to move UP before. But now ACTION7 moves LEFT. So maybe I need to use UP instead?
Let me check if UP is a valid action. Yes, it is. Let me try using UP to move the character up.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# ACTION7 seems to move toward the dark gray shape, not always UP
# Let me try using UP directly to move the character up
# Current: (48,48)-(56,56), need to get to row 45 to better overlap yellow shape
action(['UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
